oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Understanding the Impact of the Endangered Species Act
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Jordan, Rick
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 49 Number 2 (July 2022).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.Understand the Impact of the Endangered Species ActThe Endangered Species Act (ESA) was passed into law in the early 1970s. Many people believe that this law is only applicable to species indigenous to the United States, but this is not true. The ESA is not bound by the borders of this country, and the US Fish and Wildlife Service evaluates and maintains the list of Worldwide species that fit their criteria for listing. Foreign species are included and activities with any listed species may be restricted. The ESA now includes several of the parrots found in US aviaries and in the pet trade.There are two main categories of listing under the ESA, endangered or threatened. If the US Fish and Wildlife Service determines that a species qualifies for either of these categories, they are listed, and certain prohibitions are placed on the species here in the United States. Species listed as endangered or threatened are prohibited in interstate commerce and require federal permits for any sale across a state line, as well as any import or export to or from the US. The only exception is when the US Fish and Wildlife Service decides to use the 4(d) ruling; a rule that is custom designed for the species that will enhance their conservation. A 4(d) rule can only be applied to those species determined to be threatened, it is not used on any species listed as endangered. In the case of parrots, the ruling removes the need to acquire a federal permit to sell a listed species across a...Click the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 49 No. 2 (2022): Watchbird July 2022; 20-21
